1.pericope_label: Unity in the Body of Christ
1.synthesis: Paul, the prisoner of the Lord, urges them to walk in a manner worthy of the calling to which they have been called—with all humility and gentleness, with patience, bearing with one another in love, eager to maintain the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace (Ephesians 4:1–3). There is one body and one Spirit—just as they were called to the one hope that belongs to their call—one Lord, one faith, one baptism, one God and Father of all, who is over all and through all and in all (Ephesians 4:4–6). But grace was given to each one of them according to the measure of Christ's gift. Christ ascended on high, led a host of captives, and gave gifts to people (Ephesians 4:7–8; citing Psalm 68:18). He who descended is the one who also ascended far above all the heavens, that he might fill all things (Ephesians 4:9–10). He gave the apostles, prophets, evangelists, shepherds and teachers, to equip the saints for the work of ministry, for building up the body of Christ, until they all attain to the unity of the faith and of the knowledge of the Son of God, to mature manhood, to the measure of the stature of the fullness of Christ (Ephesians 4:11–13). No longer children tossed by waves and every wind of doctrine, but speaking the truth in love, they are to grow up in every way into him who is the head, into Christ—from whom the whole body, joined and held together by every joint with which it is equipped, makes the body grow so that it builds itself up in love (Ephesians 4:14–16).
1.historical_context: The seven-fold unity list of vv.4–6 (one body, Spirit, hope, Lord, faith, baptism, God and Father) is likely a liturgical or catechetical formula—a confession of unity that the community would have known by heart. The Psalm 68:18 citation in v.8 is adapted from the LXX (which differs from the Hebrew) and reread christologically: where Psalm 68 describes God ascending to Sinai and receiving tribute/gifts from humans, Paul reads Christ's ascension as his distributing gifts to his people. The 'he descended' of v.9 is interpreted variously as the incarnation (descent to earth) or as the descent to Hades (before the resurrection)—Paul's point is the one who is now exalted over all is the same one who descended to the lowest condition. The five-fold ministry list (apostles, prophets, evangelists, shepherds and teachers, v.11) is the most developed ministry-gift list in the NT, distinguishing more roles than the lists in Romans 12 and 1 Corinthians 12.
1.literary_structure: The chapter's first section (vv.1–16) moves from ethical appeal (walk worthy, vv.1–3) to theological grounding (the sevenfold unity, vv.4–6) to the christological basis of diversity in unity (Christ's ascent distributes gifts, vv.7–10) to the purpose of the ministry gifts (equipping for the body's building up, vv.11–13) to the vision of mature corporate growth (vv.14–16). The movement is from the imperative (walk worthy) through the indicative (one body, one Lord) to the teleological (until we all attain to the fullness of Christ). The body metaphor (vv.15–16) ties together the diversity of gifts (joints) and the unity of growth (the body builds itself up in love).
1.theological_application: The seven unities of vv.4–6 are not ideals to achieve but realities to maintain ('eager to maintain the unity of the Spirit,' v.3): the unity already exists in Christ; the community's calling is to live consistently with it. The church's divisions—denominational, ethnic, class-based, doctrinal—are therefore not just organizational failures but theological contradictions: they deny in practice what the one baptism, one Spirit, and one Father affirm in confession. The ministry gifts are given 'to equip the saints for the work of ministry' (v.12): the apostles, prophets, evangelists, shepherds and teachers do not do the ministry on behalf of a passive congregation but equip every member for the ministry that belongs to all. The measure of maturity is christological: 'the measure of the stature of the fullness of Christ' (v.13)—no human standard of spiritual development is adequate; only Christ himself is the measure.
1.christology: 'He who descended is the one who also ascended far above all the heavens, that he might fill all things' (v.10)—the descent-ascent pattern traces the incarnation-resurrection-exaltation of Christ in a single sentence. The one who fills all things is the same one who emptied himself in incarnation; the cosmic lordship of the exalted Christ is inseparable from the self-humbling of the one who descended. 'Speaking the truth in love, we are to grow up in every way into him who is the head, into Christ' (v.15)—Christ is both the source of the body's growth (from whom the body draws its life) and the goal of the body's growth (into whom the body is being formed). The entire ecclesiology of Ephesians is christologically shaped: the church is Christ's body (1:23), growing into Christ's fullness (4:13), under Christ's headship (4:15), filled with Christ's own love (4:16).

17.synthesis: Paul testifies in the Lord that they must no longer walk as the Gentiles do, in the futility of their minds—darkened in their understanding, alienated from the life of God because of the ignorance in them, due to their hardness of heart. They became callous and gave themselves up to sensuality, greedy to practice every kind of impurity (Ephesians 4:17–19). That is not the way they learned Christ—assuming they heard about him and were taught in him, as the truth is in Jesus, to put off the old self (which belongs to the former manner of life and is corrupt through deceitful desires), to be renewed in the spirit of their minds, and to put on the new self, created after the likeness of God in true righteousness and holiness (Ephesians 4:20–24).
17.historical_context: The description of Gentile life in vv.17–19 draws on standard Jewish polemic against pagan idolatry and its moral consequences (cf. Romans 1:18–32): the chain of darkened mind → hardened heart → spiritual blindness → moral callousness → sensuality → impurity is the diagnostic pattern of life lived without the true God. 'The futility of their minds' (mataiotes) echoes Ecclesiastes' 'vanity' (LXX: mataiotes)—existence without reference to the living God is inherently purposeless. 'As the truth is in Jesus' (v.21) is unusual—Paul typically speaks of 'the Lord Jesus Christ,' not 'Jesus' alone; the bare name may emphasize the historical particularity of the incarnate one in whom the truth is embodied, not merely proclaimed. The 'put off/put on' language (vv.22, 24) is almost certainly baptismal: the old self is stripped off at baptism as old garments; the new self is clothed at baptism as new garments (cf. Galatians 3:27: 'you who were baptized into Christ have put on Christ').
17.literary_structure: The section contrasts two ways of life: the Gentile way (futility, darkness, hardness, callousness, sensuality, vv.17–19) and the Christ-taught way (put off, be renewed, put on, vv.20–24). The pivot is 'but that is not the way you learned Christ' (v.20)—the negative contrast establishes that learning Christ involves a comprehensive reorientation of mind, character, and conduct. The three-part instruction (put off/be renewed/put on) corresponds to the three dimensions of the transformation: the past self stripped, the mind renewed, the new self clothed. The 'created after the likeness of God in true righteousness and holiness' (v.24) echoes the creation account—the new self is the re-creation of the image of God that sin had defaced.
17.theological_application: The put-off/put-on framework is not a performance-based moralism but a baptismal ontology: the old self has been crucified (Romans 6:6), and the new self has been created (2 Corinthians 5:17). The ethical exhortation is to live consistently with what has already happened: 'put off the old self' as the ongoing appropriation of the definitive stripping-off that occurred at baptism. The 'renewal of the spirit of your mind' (v.23) is the ongoing transformation of the cognitive-moral faculty by the Spirit—not willpower redirecting existing desires but the Spirit creating new desires through the renewed mind. The goal is the likeness of God in righteousness and holiness: not merely moral improvement but the restoration of the divine image that constitutes genuine human existence.
17.christology: 'You learned Christ' (v.20) is a remarkable phrase—Christ is not merely a subject of learning (content) but the teacher and the context ('in him') and the content ('as the truth is in Jesus'). The truth is not abstract doctrine but the person of Jesus in whom truth is embodied: his character, his self-giving, his death and resurrection are the truth that shapes the new self. The 'new self, created after the likeness of God in true righteousness and holiness' (v.24) is the image of the one who is himself 'the image of the invisible God' (Colossians 1:15): to be renewed after God's likeness is to be conformed to the pattern of Christ. The ethical transformation that Paul commands is participation in the new creation that Christ inaugurated—becoming, by the Spirit's work, what Christ already is in himself.

25.synthesis: Therefore, having put away falsehood, let each speak the truth with his neighbor, for they are members of one another (Ephesians 4:25; citing Zechariah 8:16). Be angry and do not sin; do not let the sun go down on your anger, and give no opportunity to the devil (Ephesians 4:26–27; citing Psalm 4:4). Let the thief no longer steal but rather labor, doing honest work with his own hands, so that he may have something to share with anyone in need (Ephesians 4:28). Let no corrupting talk come out of their mouths, but only what is good for building up, as fits the occasion, that it may give grace to those who hear (Ephesians 4:29). Do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God, by whom they were sealed for the day of redemption (Ephesians 4:30). Let all bitterness and wrath and anger and clamor and slander be put away from them, along with all malice. Be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave them (Ephesians 4:31–32).
25.historical_context: The specific vices and virtues in vv.25–32 have a communal logic: lying, anger, theft, corrupt speech, and bitterness all damage the community's unity; truth, constructive speech, generosity, and forgiveness build it up. The Zechariah citation ('speak truth with your neighbor,' v.25) comes from a context of covenant restoration—the new covenant community is characterized by the truth-speaking that marks God's own people. The 'do not grieve the Holy Spirit' (v.30) is unique in the NT as an appeal to the Spirit's own sensitivity: the Spirit can be grieved by the community's behavior, which means the Spirit is personal and relationally engaged with the community. The sealing by the Spirit 'for the day of redemption' (v.30) ties the ethical instruction to eschatological accountability: the Spirit who seals believers for final redemption is the Spirit who is grieved when the community's behavior contradicts its sealed identity.
25.literary_structure: The concrete ethical instructions (vv.25–32) apply the put-off/put-on framework of vv.17–24 to specific behaviors: each instruction has a negative (put off: falsehood, anger lingered, stealing, corrupt talk, bitterness) and a positive (put on: truth, anger resolved, honest labor with generosity, edifying speech, kindness). The motivations are communal ('members of one another,' v.25), spiritual ('give no opportunity to the devil,' v.27; 'grieve not the Spirit,' v.30), and theological ('as God in Christ forgave you,' v.32). The section closes with the christological ground of forgiveness—the ultimate motivation for the community's mutual kindness is the divine forgiveness already received.
25.theological_application: 'Do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God, by whom you were sealed for the day of redemption' (v.30) is the most serious motivational frame in the passage: the Spirit who is the community's guarantee and seal is personally affected by the community's behavior. This is not a threat of losing salvation but a relational warning: the behavior that grieves the Spirit is behavior that contradicts the community's sealed identity. The standard of forgiveness (v.32: 'as God in Christ forgave you') sets the bar at the highest possible level—the community's forgiveness of one another is measured by the divine forgiveness received, not by the offense's severity or the offender's repentance. This means Christian forgiveness is grace-based, not merit-based: given to the undeserving because it was received by the undeserving.
25.christology: 'Forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you' (v.32) is the christological ground of community ethics: the forgiveness the community extends to one another participates in and reflects the forgiveness God enacted in Christ. The cross is the normative act of forgiveness—unconditional, costly, preemptive—and those who have received that forgiveness are called to extend it on the same terms. 'In Christ' is the locative of the divine forgiveness: it was accomplished in Christ's death and resurrection, and those who are 'in Christ' share the forgiveness that is his to give. The entire ethical section (4:25–32) is grounded in the christological reality of 4:20–24: having learned Christ, having put on the new self created after God's likeness, the community now lives in the character of the one whose self-giving for others defines what it means to be truly human.
